title: Limua pahole Austin & Rubinoff 2026
author: Austin, Kyhl A.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Limua pahole  Austin & Rubinoff, sp. nov.   http://zoobank.org/ urn:lsid:zoobank.org:act: 3FCBBFB3-FEF2-48D5-AEAD-2F6F28C58ADB  Figs. 7A, B, J–L; 9A, 10A   Diagnosis.   Limua pahole  can be most easily separated from other species of  Limua  by the iridescent blue-green scaling of the FW of both sexes (iridescent green or iridescent dark green in other  Limua ). However, this character is easily lost in damaged or faded specimens. Male genitalia are unique among  Limua  in possessing a shallow basal concavity on the ventral margin of the valva (Fig. 9A). Female genitalia of  L .  pahole  are largely indistinguishable from other species of  Limua , but with so few females of  Limua  known, it is difficult to draw such conclusions about the range of variation.   Holotype,  ♂.  Oʻahu:  Pahole N[atural] A[rea] R[eserve], n[ea]r Pisonia Flats; 21.5409, -158.1928; 595 m; larva coll. 16 iv 2023, pup. 17 v 2023, adult ecl. 2 vi 2023; K. A. Austin / HOST:  Nestegis  [=  Notelaea ]  sandwicensis  (Oleaceae), initial instars feeding inside frass-lined tube, later becoming leaf-tier / DNA extraction KA0820 / KAA diss. #1108 (UHIM).  Paratypes  (5♂♂, 1♀).  Oʻahu:  1♂, Pahole N[atural] A[rea] R[eserve], in gulch; 21.5415, -158.1923; 580 m; 19–20 ii 2023; K.A. Austin; UV bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0741 / KAA diss. #0973 (UHIM). 1♀, Pahole N[atural] A[rea] R[eserve], in gulch; 21.5401, -158.1920; 610 m; 18–19 xii 2021; K.A. Austin, K. Faccenda, H. Szczygieł; UV bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0431 / KAA diss. #0687 (UHIM). 1♂, Waiʻanae Mountains, Mokulēʻia F[orest] R[eserve], 0.5 mi N of Puʻu Pane; 1800 ft [549 m]; 25 ix 1976 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 2♂♂, Waiʻanae Range, above Mākua Valley; 2200 ft [671 m]; 11 ix 1976 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♂, Waimano; 5 i [19]13; ex.  Olea  [=  Notelaea ?] / KAA diss. #0411 (HDOA).   Description. Male (n=6).     Head .  Frons clothed in dark brown scales, slightly paler on lateral margins; vertex similarly colored but with some pale green scales present medially; labial palpus ca. 1.5× diameter of compound eye, porrect or slightly ascending, lateral surface clothed in dark brown scales, medial surface straw yellow; scaling on scape glossy brown, occasionally with faded brown-green scales; sensillae porrect, ca. 1× width of flagellomere; dorsal scales of flagellum dark brown, nearly black, occasionally with scattered brownish-green scales on first few segments, ventral half of flagellum naked, serrate towards apex.   Thorax .  Scales on pronotum dark gray to brown, heavily intermixed with iridescent pale green scales, especially on anterior and posterior margins; tegulae concolorous with scaling on pronotum, iridescent green scaling restricted to medial margin. Forelegs and midlegs dark brown dorsolaterally, these dark brown scales iridescent purple under light, straw yellow on medial faces; hindlegs shining straw yellow. Dorsal surface of FW (FWL: 8.5–10.0 mm; FAR: 2.3; Fig. 7A) with ground color dark brown to black, heavily overlaid with iridescent blue-green to pale green scales between veins; banding only apparent along costa; FW costal fold present, to ca. 0.25× costa, narrow, distinct; fringe black at veins, white between. Dorsal surface of HW dark gray, strigulae broad, apparent towards apex; fringe dark gray. Ventral surface of FW similar to dorsal surface but without iridescent green scales; costa with banding broad, distinct, iridescent purple under light. Ventral surface of HW similar to dorsal surface except paler, strigulae more distinct.   Abdomen .  Vestiture of abdomen entirely dark brown to shining gray. Male genitalia (Fig. 9A) with uncus elongate, broad, quadrate, slightly flared apically, with shallowly concave on apical margin; socii obsolete, reduced to small setose mounds, scarcely differentiated from tegumen; arms of gnathos moderate, fused at apex to form broad, apically rounded plate; tegumen robust, arching; labides large, inflated, subquadrate, with short, robust, anteriorly directed spines on dorsal surface, separate, but joined medially by thin membrane; valvae broad, concave on ventral margin at base, trapezoidal, with distal margin wide, parallel to basal margin, not broadly rounded, with distinct plication on dorsal margin; juxta plate-like, with V-shaped apicoventral notch and small lateral patches of setae; phallus short, stout, without any observed cornuti nor sockets.   Description. Female (n=1).    Head.  Similar to male but scaling on vertex slightly paler than in male; flagellum thinner, without ventral serrations; sensillae shorter, sparse.  Thorax.  Similar to male except larger (FWL: 13.8 mm; FAR: 2.4; Fig. 7B); costal fold absent; with porrect, rough scaling at base of costa; iridescent green scaling on dorsal surface of FW distinctly yellow-green (as opposed to blue-green in males), largely restricted to base of FW, along costa, and as incomplete fasciae across FW; thin black medial streak from base of FW to 0.2× FW; dorsal surface of HW paler, with more strongly contrasting strigulae.  Abdomen.  Vestiture of abdomen similar to male. Female genitalia (Fig. 10A) with papillae anales broad, elliptical, separate, evenly roughened on ventral surface; apophyses posteriores ca. 0.4× S7; apophyses anteriores ca. 0.7×; sterigma wide, slender, bowl-shaped, thickened medially near ostium; ostium small, ring-like; colliculum long, faint, parallel-sided, opened on ventral margin; ductus bursae thin, straight, ca. 0.7× S7; corpus bursae large, elongate, membranous, ca. 3× length of S7; signum absent; ductus seminalis arising near base of corpus bursae.   Etymology.  From the type locality of this species, Pahole Natural Area Reserve.   Biology.   Limua pahole  has been reared from leaves of olopua ( Notelaea sandwicensis ; Oleaceae). Early instars construct a conspicuous frass-lined feeding tunnel on the abaxial surface of leaves where they proceed to skeletonize a small patch from the safety of their feeding shelter. Later instars leave this feeding shelter and proceed to tie two leaves together with silk and continue feeding inside. Pupation takes place inside this second feeding shelter.   Distribution.  With the exception of a single male collected in Waimano in the Koʻolau Mountains over a century ago,  Limua pahole  is known exclusively from the northern Waiʻanae Mountains, in and around Pahole Natural Area Reserve at elevations between ca. 550– 670 m. This area contains some of the most intact remaining mesic forest and perhaps the largest wild population of olopua anywhere on Oʻahu.   Conservation status.   Limua pahole  is likely critically endangered. Despite many surveys across Oʻahu in suitable habitat, larval feeding damage has only been observed by us in a small area of Pahole Natural Area Reserve. This is the only location where adults have been collected in recent years and even there they appear to be very rare. Larval feeding damage of  Limua  is conspicuous (Fig. 7I), and this may be used as a survey tool for  L .  pahole  on olopua elsewhere on Oʻahu in the hopes of discovering additional populations.
